Title: Self-love
ChapterText:
Self-love isn't about being perfect; it's about being on your own side when things get difficult. By cultivating self-confidence, we shift the focus from "Will they like me?" to 'I will be okay, even if they don't.
Self-love is also about doing things you are scared of, but doing anyways.

Title: Inner critic
ChapterText:
Social anxiety is often rooted in a harsh inner critic. Studies showed that self-criticism, not only low-self esteem are the strongest predictor of the severity of social anxiety.
Harsh inner critic can be linked to beliefs like: "I am not good enough","People will reject me", "They will make fun of me" etc.

These deeply rooted beliefs manifest in the way you behave. They will not dissapear on command, they will be weakened only by new positive experiences, healthy self-talk and self-acceptance. Then there will be space for new, beneficial beliefs.

Title: The Power of Self-Acceptance
ChapterText: Social anxiety often stems from the belief that we must be 'perfect' to be accepted. Self-acceptance is the brave act of letting go of that perfectionism. It is not about giving up on growth; it’s about growing from a place of kindness rather than shame. When you accept yourself, including your anxiety, you become bulletproof to the judgment of others.

Title: Mute your inner critic
ChapterText: When you feel the inner critic awakens and starts to talk confront him by replacing "What if" by "Even if".For example you might have a meeting with new people, and you are worried about what they will think of you and whether you will make good first impression. Thought like "What if they won't accept me" mind go through your mind. Attack this thought by replacing it with "Even if": "Even if they will not accept me, it doesn't say anything about my value. I am worthy as I am."
